macro_segments.title: Introduction: The Role of God's Name in Oaths and the Ra'avad's Distinction Between Types of Oaths

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and transition to the fourth perek of Masechet Edus
The centrality of God's name to oaths: comparison with neder and discussion of shevuas shav
Rambam's ruling on oaths without explicit God's name and the Ra'avad's disagreement
Ra'avad's two-component analysis: God's name for malkus and the metaphysical obligation of shevua

macro_segments.title: Shevuas Ha-Eidus: Scope and Application of the Oath of Testimony

micro_segments.title: Basic parameters of shevuas ha-eidus: applies to men, not women; distant relatives, not close; qualified witnesses
Rabbi Meir vs. Chachamim debate: does shevuas ha-eidus apply outside of court and when self-imposed?
Why shevuas ha-eidus cannot serve as a subpoena to compel court appearance
Liability framework: zadon hashvua, zadon ha-eidus, and shiggat shvua; korban oleh veyored
Discussion of perjury, false testimony, and the distinction between denying testimony and giving false testimony

macro_segments.title: Derasha from Vayikra 5:1 on Women as Witnesses: Interpretation and Implications

micro_segments.title: The pasuk 've-amdu shnei ha-anashim' interpreted as referring to witnesses rather than litigants
Three derivations in Braitot for why 'anashim' excludes women from testimony
Tosafot's question: can the word 'anashim' be merely descriptive rather than exclusionary?
Tosafot's resolution: women as witnesses must come regardless of custom; women as litigants may use representation

macro_segments.title: Courtroom Procedure: Standing, Sitting, and Equal Treatment of Litigants

micro_segments.title: Ve-amdu shnei ha-anashim as teaching that litigants stand and judges sit; Rabbi Yehuda's exception
B'tzedek tishpot amitecha: fair treatment and the distinction between equality and justice
Alternative derasha: judge your fellow favorably (l'chaf zechut) in social contexts
Rav Yosef's reading: 'amitecha refers to one's fellow in Torah and mitzvot; preference in scheduling cases

macro_segments.title: Witnesses Must Stand; Rav Huna's Wife and the Tension Between Kavod HaTorah and Equal Treatment

micro_segments.title: Rav Huna: witnesses stand always; litigants stand only at time of final judgment, not during deliberations
The case of Rav Huna's widow: Rav Nachman's dilemma between honoring Torah scholars and enforcing equal treatment
Rav Nachman's solution: releasing a goose to have a pretext to stand; Tosafot's two explanations

macro_segments.title: Witnesses Must Stand; Rav Huna's Wife and the Tension Between Kavod HaTorah and Equal Treatment (continued)

micro_segments.title: Reconciling the dilemma: sitting half out of one's chair while passing judgment

macro_segments.title: Balancing Kavod HaTorah with Equal Treatment: Cases of Torah Scholars and Laypeople as Litigants

micro_segments.title: Rabbah bar Rav Huna: allowing both Torah scholar and layperson to sit while allowing voluntary standing
Rav bar Sharvaya case: court officer forces layperson to stand; Rav Papa's justification
Rabbah bar Avuha: Torah scholar should not enter courtroom early to avoid appearance of collusion
Exception: if judge and Torah scholar have fixed learning time together, no problem

macro_segments.title: Kavod versus Protection from Sin: When Human Dignity Must Yield to Preventing Transgressions

micro_segments.title: Rabbah bar Avuha: Torah scholar witness need not testify before lower-status judges due to honor concerns
Rav Sheshet's parallel: found objects beneath one's dignity; honor may override monetary obligations
V'hani mili b'mamona: monetary context; transgressions require overriding honor considerations
Rashi's interpretation: refusing to violate a judge's ruling even if it leads to transgression
Conclusion: the tension between protecting God's honor ex post (punishment) versus ex ante (prevention)
